back: A bandwidth management technique prioritizing the flow of network traffic
Also known as packet shaping
Prioritizes higher priority traffic over lower priority traffic
A traffic shaping service that prioritizes traffic of resource-intensive applications at layer 3
Shapes and prioritizes traffic at the network layer
Allows high priority services more bandwidth than lower priority services
A traffic shaping service that prioritizes traffic of resource-intensive applications at layer 2
Classifies and prioritizes traffic by group at the data link layer
Example: Assigns higher priority to VoIP traffic and lower priority to HTTP traffic
Routes and encrypts all network traffic through the VPN regardless of destination
Generally recommended and more secure because all network traffic is encrypted
Better option for handling confidential data
Routes and encrypts all non-internet network traffic over the VPN
Internet traffic bypasses the VPN server
Less secure than full tunnel but allows simultaneous access to internet and office resources
A network topology in which more than one path exists between two network endpoints
Also known as layer 2 loop or bridge loop
Occurs at the data link layer (layer 2)
The occurrence of a large number of broadcast and multicast packets on a network within a short time period
Also known as network storm
Degrades network performance by exhausting network bandwidth
The act of preventing or reducing packet rebroadcasts on a LAN
Also known as storm control
Methods include removal of network loops by disabling ports and limiting broadcast traffic rate
A packet exchanged between switches on a local area network (LAN) to detect network loops
Used by the spanning tree protocol (STP) to construct a loop-free logical topology
Should only be sent by switches, not end devices
A control mechanism for preventing a BPDU packet from entering a switch port
Protects a layer 2 STP topology from BPDU-based attacks
Prevents attackers from injecting malicious BPDU packets
A network protocol a layer 2 device uses to eliminate a loop
IEEE standardized as IEEE 802.1D
Features: Eliminates existing loops, reconfigures network when changes occur, increases fault tolerance
A network loop eliminating protocol with faster convergence time than STP
IEEE standardized as IEEE 802.1w
Convergence time is less than ten seconds compared to STP's 30-50 seconds; backward compatible with STP
RSTP has four port states: disabled, discarding, learning, and forwarding
Discarding state: does not forward traffic; used initially to prevent network loops
Learning state: used during convergence to learn network topology; Forwarding state: forwards traffic normally after convergence
The time it takes for STP to eliminate loops and establish forwarding paths
STP convergence time: 30-50 seconds
RSTP convergence time: less than 10 seconds
